coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruizhong
Adsetts, Jonathan Ralph
Nie, Yuting
Sun, Xuhui
Ding, Zhifeng
coredata.dc:description:
Nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs) have been synthesized via one-pot pyrolysis approach using citric acid and l-Cysteine as precursors. The obtained NS-GQDs have a size around 2.8 ± 1.0 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of NS-GQDs was calculated to be 84 times that of Ru(bpy)3Cl2, which is 9.3-fold higher than that of undoped GQDs. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of NS-GQDs was found to be robust with K2S2O8 as a coreactant and an ECL efficiency of 32% relative to the Ru(bpy)3Cl2/K2S2O8 was reached, which is also 5.8-fold enhanced when compared with the same system of undoped GQDs. By means of spooling ECL spectroscopy, a potential-dependent ECL emission was discovered in the ECL processes of both NS-GQDs and GQDs. Scanning the applied potential to different values changed actually the driving force, and thus various emissive excited states were produced, leading to variable emission wavelengths. Importantly, because much more surface capturing centers of excitons for NS-GQDs than GQDs created by nitrogen and sulfur doping effect, the NS-GQDs can capture more holes from SO4
− and thereby give higher ECL efficiency. The ECL and electron transfer mechanisms proposed here provide insight into development of new GQD for light emitting devices and bioanalytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
GQDs
Heteroatom doping
Blue photoluminescence
Electrochemiluminescence
ECL
K2S2O8

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council Canada
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada
Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors sincerely thank the financial support from Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council Canada ( NSERC, DG RGPIN-2013-201697 and SPG STPGP-2016-493924 ), Canada Foundation of Innovation , Ontario Innovation Trust ( CFI/OIT, 9040 ), Premier's Research Excellence Award ( PREA, 2003 ), and Western University . The quality service from the Western Electronic Shop and ChemBio Store is greatly appreciated. We would like to thank graduate student in Dr. Yang Song's group, Shang Jiang, for her assistance in conduct IR spectroscopy. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) Low-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the as-prepared NS-GQDs. The inset of (a) shows the particle size distribution histogram, while the inset of (b) shows the high-resolution TEM image of NS-GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) N1s, (c) C1s and (d) S2p of the NS-GQDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of citric acid (blue), l-Cysteine (red) and NS-GQDs (black). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence spectra of NS-GQDs in aqueous solution (0.3 mg mL−1), λex = 350 nm and λem = 430 nm. The insets show the photographs of the obtained NS-GQDs in aqueous solution under illumination of daylight (left) and UV (365 nm, right). (b) PL spectra of the NS-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Differential pulse voltammograms for NS-GQDs modified GCE (solid red) and bare GCE (dash black). (b and c) Cyclic voltammograms and the corresponding ECL-voltage curves of NS-GQDs in the absence (b) and presence (c) of 25 mM K2S2O8. The supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Spooling ECL spectra acquired at a time interval of 1 s or a potential interval of 50 mV and (b) the corresponding cyclic voltammogram for a NS-GQDs modified GCE in the presence of 25 mM K2S2O8 in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 0.05 V s−1 during a potential scan cycle between 0.2 V and −2.8 V. The insets in (a) show stack spectra of spooling for clarity of the corresponding wavelength where the same color-coded ECL evolution and devolution are illustrated. Color codes: blue (forward scan, 0.2 to −2.3 V), red (forward scan, −2.3 to −2.8 V) and black (backward scan, −2.8 to 0.2 V). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The University of Western Ontario, London, ON N6A 5B7, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs) have been synthesized via one-pot pyrolysis approach using citric acid and l-Cysteine as precursors. The obtained NS-GQDs have a size around 2.8 ± 1.0 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of NS-GQDs was calculated to be 84 times that of Ru(bpy)3Cl2, which is 9.3-fold higher than that of undoped GQDs. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of NS-GQDs was found to be robust with K2S2O8 as a coreactant and an ECL efficiency of 32% relative to the Ru(bpy)3Cl2/K2S2O8 was reached, which is also 5.8-fold enhanced when compared with the same system of undoped GQDs. By means of spooling ECL spectroscopy, a potential-dependent ECL emission was discovered in the ECL processes of both NS-GQDs and GQDs. Scanning the applied potential to different values changed actually the driving force, and thus various emissive excited states were produced, leading to variable emission wavelengths. Importantly, because much more surface capturing centers of excitons for NS-GQDs than GQDs created by nitrogen and sulfur doping effect, the NS-GQDs can capture more holes from SO4
− and thereby give higher ECL efficiency. The ECL and electron transfer mechanisms proposed here provide insight into development of new GQD for light emitting devices and bioanalytical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence is the generation of light without heat. As pointed out in published papers, light can be emitted through many luminescent processes, which include photoluminescence (PL), chemiluminescence (CL) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [1,2]. Analogous to CL, ECL is a redox-induced light emission, in which high-energy species, generated at electrodes, undergo an electron transfer reaction forming an excited state that emit light [3–5]. The excited state can be produced through the reaction of cation and anion radicals generated from the same luminophore, as the so-called annihilation mechanism, or from two different precursors (luminophore and co-reactant), via co-reactant mechanism. The smart combination of chemiluminescence and electrochemistry gives ECL many potential advantages, such as ECL does not require the use of external light sources, the time and position of ECL emitting can be controlled by changing an electrode potential, and ECL can be more selective than chemiluminescence because the generation of excited states in ECL can be selectively controlled by varying the electrode potentials [6–8]. Therefore, ECL is becoming an increasing popular technique in the fields of light-emitting devices and bioanalysis. The most employed ECL luminophore is ruthenium(II)tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) because of its luminescence efficiency, reversible electrochemical behavior, chemical stability and versatility [9–11]. The high cost may be the major issue to limit the wide ECL applications of Ru(bpy)3 2+. Developing new luminophores with low-cost and fine-tuned optoelectronic properties is imperative for ECL applications.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a kind of 0D small pieces of graphene with size below 20 nm [12–14], are expected to have a huge impact in biotechnological and environmental applications, based on their high potential as a nontoxic, fluorescent alternative to the traditional semiconductor-based quantum dots [15–17].In addition, their superiority in terms of low-cost, high water solubility, chemical inertness, facile modification and high resistance to photobleaching have sparked excitement in their analytical and bioanalytical applications [18,19]. However, bare GQDs without passivation of surface functionalization are demonstrated with a low quantum yield [20]. Heteroatom doping has been considered as an efficient approach to tune the optical characteristics, surface states and local chemical features of GQDs [21–23]. And these fine-tuned GQDs have been used as ECL emitter to detect small molecules, organic compound, proteins [23–25]. However, efficient one-step strategies for the large-scale production of self-functionalized GQDs from renewable precursors with inexpensive, green and fast methods are still challenging. Moreover, current GQDs and carbon quantum dots-based ECL studies mainly focused on application exploitation, but the study and illustration of electron-transfer information and ECL emission mechanisms are scarce. In this paper, we report the synthesis of the nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs) via a facile and fast one-pot pyrolysis method using citric acid and l-Cysteine as precursors. The HRTEM results revealed the as-prepared NS-GQDs have a size around 1.5–5.5 nm. The FT-IR and XPS characterization demonstrated that multiple C-, N-, S- and O-containing functional groups on the surface of the obtained NS-GQDs. Photoluminescence and UV–visible absorption spectroscopy studies showed a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 84 times that of Ru(bpy)3Cl2. The ECL of the as-prepared NS-GQDs in the presence of K2S2O8 was investigated. ECL mechanisms and electron-transfer processes were interrogated using ECL-voltage curves and spooling ECL spectroscopy. The ECL of the NS-GQDs showed a unique potential-dependent emission characteristic and a relative ECL efficiency as high as 32%. Our work might stimulate ECL investigations of plenty of graphene quantum dots and pave the way toward efficient and low cost ECL emitters for applications described above.

Citric acid[HOC(COOH)(CH2COOH)2, 99%], l-Cysteine [HSCH2CH(NH2)COOH, ≥97%], potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, 99.99%), sodium phosphate monobasic dehydrate (NaH2PO4 ·2H2O, ≥99%) and tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)-dichlororuthenium(Ⅱ) hexahydrate [Ru(bpy)3Cl2 ·6H2O, 97%] were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Mississauga, ON). Sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4, anhydrous, ≥99%) was obtained from Caledon Laboratory Chemicals (Georgetown, ON). Potassium chloride (KCl, 99%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA). Carboxymethylchitosan [(C10H19NO6)n, 99%] was obtained from Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Inc. (Dallas, TX). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Milli-Q, Millipore) was used to prepare solutions. All chemical reagents were used as received and stored at room temperature with exception of Carboxymethylchitosan that was stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NS-GQDs were synthesized via a one-pot high temperature pyrolysis method. Typically, 1.0 g citric acid and 0.3 g l-Cysteine were firstly mixed in a 25 mL round-bottom flask. Then the flask was heated at 240 °C using a heating mantle. About 2 min later, the mixture fully liquated. Subsequently, the liquid color changed from colorless to pale yellow and finally orange in about 4 min. The obtained liquid was then dissolved into 10 mL ultrapure water. The resultant orange solution was further dialyzed for 8 h by using dialysis bag with molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1000 Da against ultrapure water for purification. Finally, the product in the dialysis bag was dried through a freezing dryer.
For comparison, undoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by using single citric acid as a carbon source with the same procedure that for NS-GQDs preparation.

Low- and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were carried out on a JEM-2010 (HR) microscope operated at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by means of a VG Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific, United Kingdom) with Al Kα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation operated at 120 W. The binding energies were calibrated against the carbon 1s line. Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a VERTEX 70 FTIR. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded over a range of 200–900 nm using a Varian Cary 50 spectrophotometer (Varian Inc., North Carolina), while photoluminescence experiments were conducted by using a Fluorolog spectrophotometer (QM-7/2005, Photon Technology International, London, ON) with an excitation slit width of 0.25 mm and an emission slit width of 0.1 mm using a 10 mm quartz cuvette. The photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (ΦPL) was determined using the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ
P L
=
I x
I
s t
A
s t
A x
(
η x
η
s t
)
2
× 100 %
in which I is the integrated photoluminescence emission intensity, A is the optical density and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “st” refers to standard and “x” refers to the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) analyte [26]. The relative standard employed was Ru(bpy)3Cl2 in ultrapure water.

A specially customized photoelectrochemical cell, with a flat Pyrex window at the bottom for detection of the light generated from the working electrode, was used for all the electrochemical and ECL measurements. A three-electrode electrochemical cell system, in which a commercial glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) modified with NS-GQDs (or GQDs) was used as the working electrode, and two Pt wires were employed as counter and quasi-reference electrodes, respectively, with a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl as the electrolyte. The dissolved oxygen has a quenching effect in our current NS-GQDs ECL system (see Fig. S1 in Supplementary Data). Before the electrochemical tests, the electrolyte was purged with Ar for 15 min. The NS-GQDs (or GQDs) ink was first prepared by ultrasonically dispersing 5 mg of NS-GQDs (or GQDs) powder into 1 mL ultrapure water. The NS-GQDs (or GQDs)-modified working electrode was then fabricated by casting 20 μL of the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) ink onto the surface of the freshly polished GCE, followed by drying at room temperature. Afterward, the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) layer was covered with a thin layer of Chitosan (0.2 mg mL−1 in water, 5 μL) to ensure that the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) layer was tightly attached onto the electrode surface during the electrochemical measurements. After each experiment, the electrochemical potential window was calibrated using ferrocenemethanol as the internal standard. The redox potential of the ferrocenemethanol/ferroceniummethanol (Fc/Fc+) couple (∼1 mM internal standard) in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl was calibrated as 0.25 V vs SCE.

The voltammetric ECL curves were obtained using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 610A, CH Instruments, Austin TX) coupled with a photomultiplier tube (PMT, R928, Hamamatsu, Japan) held at −750 V with a high-voltage power supply. The ECL generated at the working electrode was collected by the PMT under the flat Pyrex window at the bottom of the electrochemical cell. The photocurrent from the PMT was transferred into a voltage signal by a picoammeter (Keithley 6487, Cleveland, OH). This signal, along with the potential and current signals from the electrochemical workstation were simultaneously sent through a DAQ board (DAQ 6052E, National Instruments, Austin TX) to the computer where the entire data acquisition system was controlled by a homemade LabVIEW (National Instruments) program.

Spooling ECL spectra were measured by placing the electrochemical cell into a spectrograph (Spectra Physics, Cornerstone 260 M) with a CCD camera (Andor 420BV) cooled to −55 °C. The exposure time and the number of kinetic series were optimized to produce the clearest ECL spectra. During all experiments, lights in the experimentation room were turned off to reduce the background interference. Blackout curtains were also positioned at the entryways to the lab and surrounding the electrochemical cell setup to prevent light interference. Wavelength calibration was conducted using a mercury-argon source (Ocean Optics, HG-1).

ECL efficiency (Φ ECL , %) was determined by comparing the ratio of the integrated ECL intensity to the charge of the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) analyte in reference to that of Ru(bpy)3Cl2/K2S2O8, using Eq. (2):
(2)
Φ
E C L
=
(
∫
E C L d t
∫
C u r r e n t d t
)
x
(
∫
E C L d t
∫
C u r r e n t d t
)
s t
× 100 %
where “ECL” and “Current” represent ECL intensity and electrochemical current values, respectively, “st” refers to the Ru(bpy)3Cl2/K2S2O8 standard and “x” refers to the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) analyte.

Due to its rich functional groups (COOH and OH), citric acid has been demonstrated as a facile precursor to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) containing abundant small sp2 clusters that is isolated within the whole graphite structure [27]. In this work, a heteroatom doping approach is employed to further tune the optical characteristics, surface states and local chemical features of GQDs, in which l-Cysteine was introduced as an additional nitrogen and sulfur precursor. Both citric acid and l-Cysteine are economic and rich in multifunctional groups (OH, COOH, SH, NH2), providing both carbon and nitrogen/sulfur sources for the formation of high quantum yield nitrogen- and sulfur-doped GQDs (NS-GQDs) by a simple one-pot pyrolysis method (details see Experimental Section). The morphology of the NS-GQDs was characterized by low- and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 1 a and b shows the representative TEM and HRTEM images of the NS-GQDs dispersed in water.

It can be observed that the formed NS-GQDs mostly spherical dots that are uniformly dispersed. Two hundred NS-GQDs were randomly selected to measure the particle size, and from the particle size distribution histogram exhibited in Fig. 1a inset, the as-prepared NS-GQDs exhibit an average size of around 2.8 ± 1.0 nm. The HRTEM image of a single NS-GQDs (inset, Fig. 1b) reveals the well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of about 0.23 nm, corresponding well to the (1120) lattice planes of graphene [18,28–30].

The composition and surface chemistry of prepared NS-GQDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron microscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Fig. 2 a shows the survey XPS spectrum of NS-GQDs. The four predominant peaks at 159.75, 281.55, 397.05 and 528.65 eV correspond to the binding energies of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. This result demonstrates that the produced NS-GQDs are mainly composed of four elements of C, N, S and O. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2a, the contents of the four elements in the NS-GQDs were determined to be 57%, 5.2%, 4.4% and 33.1%, respectively. The presence of nitrogen and sulfur confirms the successful doping of GQDs using l-Cysteine as source via the developed one-pot pyrolysis reaction. The high resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2c) demonstrated four distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, 287.8 eV and 288.4 eV, which can be attributed to CC/CC in aromatic rings, COH/CO/CN, CO and OCO groups [31,32]. The wide peak with binding energy at 284.8 eV confirmed the graphitic structure (sp2 CC) of obtained NS-GQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2b) revealed the presence both of OCN and CN (399.92 eV) functional groups [33–35]. And the presence of CSC unit was revealed in the high-resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. 2d) [36–38]. These results depicted that a great deal of O-, N- and S-containing groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine and sulfhydryl functional groups exist on the surface of GQDs, which contributed to excellent water solubility of the as-prepared NS-GQDs. The result of FT-IR further confirmed the high occupation of oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur-rich groups (Fig. 2e). The broad absorption bands at 3050–3500 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH, indicating that there are lots of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface, which again endow the GQDs good hydrophilic property. The bands at 1710, 1525 and 1420 cm−1 were attributed to the vibrational absorption bands of CO in COOH and OCNH, respectively. The broad absorption band at 2555 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of SH, while the formation of CN and CS bonds were evidenced by absorption peaks around 1195 cm−1. All these further demonstrated that nitrogen and sulfur not only functionalized on the surface of GQDs but also doping into the lattice of graphitic carbon skeleton, all which make a difference for optical characteristics of as-prepared NS-GQDs.

Fig. 3 a displays the UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence spectra of 0.3 mg mL−1 water-soluble NS-GQDs. A typical absorption band centered at around 350 nm was observed with a small band at about 250 nm. The absorption band at 250 nm can be ascribed to the π–π∗ transitions of the aromatic CC sp2 domains which can not produce evident fluorescence signal. Whereas the band-gap transition centered at 350 nm are from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which can result in strong fluorescence [33,39]. Accordingly, as shown in Fig. 3a, there are two excitation peaks around 250 and 350 nm. When excited at 250 nm, nearly no observed fluorescence emission (Fig. 3b), while strong photoluminescence emission centered at 430 nm can be observed when excited at 350 nm (Fig. 3a and b). From the photographs shown in Fig. 3a insets, the diluted NS-GQDs aqueous solution is pale orange under ambient daylight but displays a very intense blue color when irradiated with a UV light (365 nm), further suggesting the blue fluorescent property of the NS-GQDs. Moreover, it is interesting to find that NS-GQDs exhibits excitation-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence in diluted aqueous solution (Fig. 3b).

Such excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior is related to the different surface states [40]. From the XPS and FT-IR studies, there are multiple C-, N-, S- and O-containing functional groups on the surface of the obtained NS-GQDs, resulting in various surface states with different energy levels and thus a series of emissive traps. Under different excitation wavelength, the corresponding surface state emissive trap will be dominant, giving excitation-dependent fluorescence. The strongest emission of NS-GQDs was observed centered at 430 nm under 350 nm excitation. By using Ru(bpy)3Cl2 as a reference, the quantum yield of NS-GQDs was calculated to be 84 times that of Ru(bpy)3Cl2 according to Eq. (1). It should be point out that the synthesized water-soluble NS-GQDs exhibit high stability at room temperature. Even after being stored for six months in air, there is no observed of any floating or precipitated nanoparticles. For comparison, Fig. S2 in Supplementary Data also shows the UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence spectra of 0.3 mg mL−1 water-soluble undoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by using single citric acid as precursor. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of GQDs (Fig. S2a) demonstrates a broad peak around 320 nm from the excited state energy of the surface states. The photoluminescence spectra of GQDs (Fig. S2a and b) also exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission characteristics. The strongest photoluminescence emission of GQDs centered at 475 nm when excited at 390 nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of excitation spectra for NS-GQDs and GQDs were determined to be very approximate values of 61.7 nm and 60 nm, respectively, implying the similar size distribution of NS-GQDs and GQDs [33,41]. The size and surface status are critical factors in determining the photoluminescence of the carbon quantum dots [42]. So in current work the photoluminescence difference between NS-GQDs and GQDs mainly resulted from the different surface states of nitrogen and sulfur doping effects. It has been revealed that surface states created by surface functionalization serve as capturing centers of excitons, thereby giving rise to the surface-state-related fluorescence [43–45]. The green photoluminescence of GQDs (emission at 475 nm) likely originated from the surface states with oxygen-containing groups (OH, CO and COOH) [46], whereas the blue photoluminescence of NS-GQDs (emission at 430 nm) is controlled by the inherent surface states of multiple N- and S-containing functional groups (as demonstrated in XPS and FT-IR) except for O-containing functional groups [47]. In addition, the quantum yield of GQDs was calculated to be 9 times that of Ru(bpy)3Cl2 according to Eq. (1), which is 9.3-fold lower than that of NS-GQDs. The more surface emissive sites created via nitrogen and sulfur doping may be the main reason why NS-GQDs has higher quantum yield.

First, the electrochemistry of NS-GQDs modified GCE in the supporting electrolyte of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl was conducted to explore the reactivity of electrogenerated species towards the formation of ECL-active excited species by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV), and representative traces are shown in Fig. 4 a and b, respectively. DPV better displays the redox behavior due to suppression of the background current. During cathodic scanning of the applied potential, two irreversible reduction waves are observed with formal potentials of −1.44 and −1.65 V. While the anodic scan shows the two quasi-reversible oxidation waves with formal potentials at 1.26 and 1.39 V. Herein, it should be noted that the applied potential range, that is, −1.95 to 1.7 V (see Fig. 4a and b), is quite large for an aqueous solution. Under this potential condition, partial electrolysis of the solvent (i.e. water) into O2 and H2 may occur, and result in large anodic and cathodic current (Fig. 4a and b). Generally, high current densities may lead to obvious uncompensated iRu drop, i.e. high overpotential. Therefore, in the present study, 0.1 M KCl was added into the 0.1 M PBS for increasing the conductivity of solution, but the uncompensated iRu drop was still significant, this loss of high conductivity may be resulted from the high-content doping level of nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen in NS-GQDs [48–50] and the casted Chitosan film on the solid/electrolyte interface [51–56]. However, compared with the featureless DPV of bare GCE (black dash lines, Fig. 4a), the evident electrochemical characteristics appeared for NS-GQDs modified GCE (red solid lines, Fig. 4a) indicating the peaks produced in cathodic and anodic scanning potential region ascribed to the reduction and oxidation of NS-GQDs.

The electrochemiluminescence (ECL) behaviors of the obtained NS-GQDs were further investigated. The ECL of the above solution was then measured upon continuous electrochemical oxidation and reduction between the potentials determined by CV and DPV. No appreciable ECL signal was observed (blue curve, Fig. 4b), presumably due to the short lifetimes of the electrogenerated species in the vicinity of the working electrode via this annihilation pathway, where electrogenerated NS-GQDs cation radical (NS-GQDs
+ ) and anion radical (NS-GQDs −) react, and NS-GQDs∗ excited state is produced, emitting light upon relaxation to the ground state.

Given the weak ECL for NS-GQDs in the radical annihilation pathway noted above, the K2S2O8 was then added as a coreactant in an effort to enhance the ECL intensity. Figs. 4c and S3 display the NS-GQDs- and GQDs-modified GCE, and bare GCE in the 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl with the addition of 25 mM K2S2O8. The maximum ECL intensity of NS-GQDs with 25 mM K2S2O8 is about 430 nA (Fig. 4c), which is 2.3- and 78-fold higher than those of GQDs (190 nA, Fig. S3) and bare GCE (6 nA, Fig. S3 inset). This result indicates carbon quantum dots can serve as an efficient luminophore to produce ECL. While with the similar sizes of GQDs and NS-GQDs, the NS-GQDs exhibits much higher ECL intensity due to its more surface emissive sites created through nitrogen and sulfur doping effects (as demonstrated from its higher photoluminescence quantum yield). The ECL intensity of NS-GQDs with 25 mM K2S2O8 is also much greater than that in its annihilation pathway (Fig. 4b), agreeing well with previous reports that K2S2O8 has great promise as an oxidizing species to enforce stronger ECL activity of carbon quantum dots [57–62]. Moreover, it can be observed from Fig. 4c that the ECL has an onset potential of about −1.55 V and reached the first ECL peak at −2.3 V (blue curve, Fig. 4c). When scanning the potential towards the more negative values, the ECL shows gradually increased trend and reach the maximum value at −2.8 V (red curve, Fig. 4c).

Upon scanning the potential in the reverse direction, the ECL intensity decreased due to the concentration depletion of K2S2O8 (black curve, Fig. 4c). The ECL efficiency of the NS-GQDs/K2S2O8 and GQDs/K2S2O8 coreactant systems were calculated relative to the Ru(bpy)3Cl2/K2S2O8 based on the number of photos generated by the number of electrons injected. According to Eq. (2), the relative ECL efficiency of NS-GQDs/K2S2O8 (25 mM) was determined to be 32%, which is 5.8-fold higher than that of GQDs (5.5%). Also it can be noticed that a wide applied potential window required for this K2S2O8 coreactant ECL pathway. In order to eliminate the ECL contribution resulting from some possible side reactions between K2S2O8 itself and substances in this wide applied potential window, the ECL behaviors of bare GCE in the absence and presence of 25 mM K2S2O8 was collected. As shown in Fig. S3 inset, a negligible ECL signal of 6 nA was detected from K2S2O8 itself and no appreciable ECL signal from bare GCE, indicating the enhanced ECL of NS-GQDs-modified GCE owing to the interaction between NS-GQDs and K2S2O8. Thus the wide potential window in this coreactant process still could be due to the high overpotential derived from high-content doping level of NS-GQDs and the casted Chitosan film in solid/electrolyte interface. The similar results were also found in ECL emission process of other luminophores in aqueous solution [61–64].

Fig. 5 a exhibits the spooling ECL spectra of NS-GQDs modified GCE in the presence of 25 mM K2S2O8 in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The spectra were collected along with a potential scan cycle between −2.8 V and 0.2 V vs SCE (Fig. 5b). Spooling ECL spectroscopy involves the real-time monitoring of ECL during a potential scan, allowing the visualization of the evolution and devolution of the ECL in the potentiodynamic process, which has been extensively employed for getting insight into the ECL mechanisms of luminophores in our group [65–68]. The spooling ECL spectra of NS-GQDs demonstrate three distinguishable ECL features, which have been color-coded in blue (forward scan, 0.2 to −2.3 V), red (forward scan, −2.3 to −2.8 V) and black (backward scan, −2.8 to 0.2 V). A proposed mechanism for the generation of ECL is shown in Eqs. (3)–(7). It can be noticed that the onset of ECL was observed near −1.55 V with a peak wavelength of 890 nm. At this potential the NS-GQDs was reduced to the corresponding radical anion (NS-GQDs −) (Red1, -1.44 V, Fig. 4a). At a close potential (onset and peak potentials at −1.1 V and −1.9 V, Fig. 5b), K2S2O8 was also reduced to produce a strong oxidizing species, SO4
−, which then react with the negatively charged NS-GQDs (NS-GQDs −) by injecting a hole into the HOMO of NS-GQDs −, producing an excited state of NS-GQDs∗, which relaxed to the ground state and light emission [24,60,64,69–71]. Scanning potential towards the more negative values generated more SO4
−, which increased the
(3)
(4)
(5)
(6)
(7)
N S − G Q D
s ∗
→ N S − G Q D s + h ν

NS-GQDs∗ and led to ECL evolution (blue spectra, Fig. 5a). The ECL emission reached the first maximum at t = 50 s (−2.3 V), while the ECL emission blue-shifted to 705 nm. At higher negative potential the second maximum was recorded at t = 60 s (−2.8 V) with further more blue-shifted ECL emission wavelength of 660 nm (red spectra, Fig. 5a). NS-GQDs underwent the second reduction at −1.65 V (Red2, Fig. 4a) to generate new NS-GQDs −, which reacted with SO4
− producing NS-GQDs∗. This second maximum is just the point at which the electrogenerated NS-GQDs− and SO4 − concentrations reached an optimum balance to generate emissive excited states of NS-GQDs∗ via a single electron transfer. Upon scanning the potential in the reverse direction, the ECL intensity decreased due to the depletion and reverse conversion of the SO4
− and NS-GQDs species. The ECL evolution and devolution patterns demonstrated by spooling spectroscopy are matched well with the ECL-voltage curve shown in Fig. 4c. Fig. S4 also shows the spooling ECL spectra of GQDs modified GCE in the presence of 25 mM K2S2O8 in the same potential window. It is noted that the spooling ECL spectra for GQDs-modified GCE/K2S2O8 system are similar to these for NS-GQDs-modified GCE/K2S2O8 system (Fig. 5a), suggesting the similar ECL generation mechanisms existed in both NS-GQDs/K2S2O8 and GQDs/K2S2O8 systems. Namely, cathodically scanning the potential from 0.2 to −2.8 V, both K2S2O8 and luminophore (GQDs or NS-GQDs) were reduced to produce SO4
− and NS-GQDs − (or GQDs −), then SO4
− injected a hole into the HOMO of NS-GQDs − (or GQDs −), producing an excited state of NS-GQDs∗ (or GQDs∗), which relaxed to the ground state and light emission. Because much more surface capturing centers of excitons (as demonstrated by its higher photoluminescence quantum yield) for NS-GQDs created by nitrogen and sulfur doping effect, the NS-GQDs can capture more holes from SO4
− and thereby gives higher ECL efficiency.

Also noteworthy is the characteristics of the ECL emission wavelengths for both NS-GQDs/K2S2O8 and GQDs/K2S2O8 systems were shifted during the whole potential scan, which is caused by the multiple C-, N-, S- and O-containing functional groups on the surface of the obtained NS-GQDs and oxygen-containing groups (OH, CO and COOH) on the surface of GQDs, resulting in various surface states with different energy levels and thus a series of emissive traps. Scanning potentials in different values giving different driving force, thus the different emissive traps of NS-GQDs∗ (GQDs∗) produced, resulting in different emission wavelengths [61]. This result agrees well with their excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence characteristics of both NS-GQDs and GQDs. The maximum ECL emission wavelength (660 nm) was red-shifted from PL maximum (430 nm), the ECL emission (700 nm) of GQDs exhibited the similar red-shifted characteristic relative to its PL emission (475 nm), all which may be ascribed to the energy separations of the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) surface states (for ECL emission) are smaller than the band gaps of the NS-GQDs (or GQDs) core (for PL emission) [61,62].

In summary, nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs) were successfully prepared via a fast and facile one-pot pyrolysis method using citric acid and l-Cysteine as precursors. The as-prepared NS-GQDs have a size around 1.5–5.5 nm. FT-IR and XPS characterization demonstrated that the multiple C-, N-, S- and O-containing functional groups exist on the surface of obtained NS-GQDs, leading to excitation wavelength-dependent blue photoluminescence property and the maximum excitation and emission locate at 350 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield of NS-GQDs was 84 times that of Ru(bpy)3Cl2, which is 9.3-fold higher than that of undoped GQDs. Importantly, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of NS-GQDs was found to be robust with K2S2O8 as a coreactant and a relative ECL efficiency as high as 32% was reached, which is also 5.8-fold enhanced when compared with the same system of GQDs. By means of spooling ECL spectroscopy, potential-dependent ECL emissions were involved in the ECL emission processes of both as-prepared NS-GQDs and GQDs. Combining with photoluminescence study, the possible mechanism of this potential-dependent ECL emission was proposed that the multiple C-, N-, S- and O-containing functional groups on the surface of the obtained NS-GQDs and oxygen-containing groups (OH, CO and COOH) on the surface of GQDs result in various surface states with different energy levels and thus a series of emissive traps. Scanning potentials in different values acted as various driving force, and thus the different emissive traps of excited states (NS-GQDs∗ or GQDs∗) were produced, leading to variable emission wavelengths. Importantly, because much more surface capturing centers of excitons for NS-GQDs than GQDs created by nitrogen and sulfur doping effect, the NS-GQDs can capture more holes from SO4
− and thereby gives higher ECL efficiency. The ECL and electron transfer mechanisms proposed for NS-GQDs provide insight into development of new GQD for studying energy and electron transfer mechanisms possibly involved in light emitting devices and bioanalytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors sincerely thank the financial support from Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council Canada (NSERC, DG RGPIN-2013-201697 and SPG STPGP-2016-493924), Canada Foundation of Innovation, Ontario Innovation Trust (CFI/OIT, 9040), Premier's Research Excellence Award (PREA, 2003), and Western University. The quality service from the Western Electronic Shop and ChemBio Store is greatly appreciated. We would like to thank graduate student in Dr. Yang Song's group, Shang Jiang, for her assistance in conduct IR spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.11.091.
